what_manipulated: presentation format of a scenario (agent with or without moral motivation)
what_measured: willingness to attribute moral judgment to an agent
experiment: Units read scenarios describing an agent with moral understanding but lacking moral motivation (or control scenarios). The study varied whether the outcome variable was attributed moral understanding versus moral belief. Multiple survey designs tested these conditions across different participant groups. Comparisons examined whether subjects attributed moral judgments more readily under some framings than others, using descriptive statistics and proportional analysis across conditions.

what_measured: client perception of therapist alliance activity; relationship to alliance quality and session outcomes
experiment: Units (170 clients across 42 therapists) completed a new measure assessing their perceptions of therapist behaviors related to alliance. Factor analysis identified four subscales reflecting monitoring relationship, goal-setting, progress tracking, and feedback avoidance. Multilevel models tested associations between subscales and client-rated alliance and session outcomes, accounting for therapist clustering.

what_measured: association between issue agendas in newspapers, television, and political advertising
experiment: Content analysis coded issue agendas in newspaper coverage, television coverage, and political advertising from a campaign. Cross-lagged correlations tested directional influence between agendas at successive time points. Pearson correlations quantified strength of intermedia and advertising-media associations.

what_manipulated: type of stimulus (faces vs houses); probe type and delay
what_measured: recognition accuracy and matching accuracy for complete stimuli versus parts
experiment: Units performed 2-alternative-forced-choice recognition and matching tasks on stimuli (faces or houses) presented as wholes or parts. The study tested for a complete-over-part advantage by comparing accuracy across conditions. Multiple experiments manipulated probe type, delay, and procedural factors (precueing, feature naming) to assess whether holistic processing was mandatory or flexible.

what_manipulated: none (observational review of prior empirical work on moral-action-intentionality bias)
what_measured: folk attributions of intentionality as influenced by moral considerations
experiment: Narrative review synthesizing empirical literature on how moral judgments bias folk ascriptions of intentional action. The review examined whether immorality of acts affects perceived mens rea in legal contexts. Analysis used an affective model of blame attribution to explain biasing mechanisms and discussed implications for jury impartiality.

what_manipulated: morphological structure of word flankers (transparent suffix, pseudo-suffix, non-suffix, unrelated)
what_measured: lexical decision accuracy and latency to target word
experiment: Units made lexical decisions to a central target word flanked by related or unrelated words. Conditions varied whether flankers shared morphological structure (transparent suffix, pseudo-morphological, or no structure) or were orthographically or phonologically unrelated. Reaction time and accuracy compared facilitation across morphological versus non-morphological conditions to isolate the effect of transparent structure.

what_manipulated: range of response scale categories (low range: 0-2.5 hours vs high range: 0-4.5+ hours)
what_measured: self-reported daily behavior; subsequent judgment of average behavior and evaluation of that behavior
experiment: Units reported television use on scales with different category ranges, then made judgments about average usage and evaluated the behavior. Manipulation of scale range tested whether response categories served as anchors affecting both reported behavior and downstream judgments. Comparisons examined behavior reports, average-use estimates, and evaluations of importance/satisfaction across scale conditions.

what_manipulated: number and structure of preceding specific questions before a general question
what_measured: judgment to the general question (assimilation or contrast relative to specific-question priming)
experiment: Units answered a general question preceded by either one specific question, multiple specific questions, or no preceding question. The abstract-response predictions depended on whether questions were perceived as belonging to the same conversational context. Analysis tested whether preceding questions caused assimilation (information reuse) or contrast (nonredundancy reinterpretation) effects on the general judgment using comparison of means or effect sizes.

what_manipulated: presence and characteristics of commercial interruptions (ad quality, interruption type)
experiment: Units watched television programs with or without commercial interruptions (varying in quality and type). Six studies tested enjoyment ratings across conditions and explored boundary conditions (adaptation proneness, program type). Comparisons examined whether interruptions affected program enjoyment through a proposed adaptation-disruption mechanism, with moderation by individual differences and stimulus properties.

what_manipulated: none (observational analysis of wage gaps by firm size and job responsibility)
what_measured: wage gaps as a function of job responsibility level
experiment: Panel data from Sweden and occupational cross-section from US were analyzed using fixed-effects regression models. Outcomes (wage gaps by firm size) were regressed on predictor (job responsibility measured via direct measures or supervisory status). Analysis tested whether wage gaps increased with responsibility level and compared patterns across blue-collar and white-collar workers.

what_manipulated: variety in an assortment (identical vs varied colors/shapes) and context salience
what_measured: perceived quantity of items in the assortment
experiment: Units estimated quantity of dots or shapes displayed in patterns with varied or identical colors/geometric properties. Additional studies tested whether perceived quantity affected pouring behavior when using varied items to match a reference sample. Manipulations tested salience of overall area and number of item types. Comparisons used quantity estimates and measured poured amounts across conditions.

what_manipulated: source expertise (high vs low) and source certainty (high vs low)
what_measured: persuasion and consumer involvement with message
experiment: Units received persuasive messages from sources varying in expertise and expressed certainty. Three experiments manipulated these factors and measured persuasion (attitude change or choice) and involvement (attention or cognitive engagement). Predictions tested an incongruity hypothesis: low-expertise sources gain persuasiveness through certainty; high-expertise sources through uncertainty. Comparisons examined persuasion across source-certainty conditions.

what_manipulated: system threat (attack on legitimacy) and individual confidence in system legitimacy
what_measured: consumption choice (preference for direct vs indirect system defense)
experiment: Units' confidence in system legitimacy was measured. All units then received a threat (verbal attack) to the system. Consumption choices were presented allowing direct or indirect defense mechanisms. The study tested whether threat activates defense motivation in all units but that individuals differing in confidence adopt different defense strategies. Comparisons examined choice patterns and preferences across confidence levels after threat.

what_manipulated: product orientation in visual depiction (toward dominant hand vs away)
what_measured: purchase intention; secondary: attention resources in embodied simulation
experiment: Units viewed advertisements with products oriented to facilitate or limit embodied mental simulation (e.g., hand-dominant orientation). Four studies measured purchase intentions and tested predictions about motor response activation. Boundary conditions included perceptual resource occupation and product valence (positive vs negative). Comparisons examined purchase intentions across depiction conditions and tested mediation through mental simulation.

what_manipulated: horizontal position of product image in advertisement (congruent vs incongruent with left-right time mental model)
experiment: Units from left-to-right reading cultures viewed ads with products positioned left or right. Four studies measured attitude toward time-related or time-attributed products. Manipulations tested congruence with the left (past) right (future) mental timeline. Mediation by processing fluency and moderation by need for structure were tested. Comparisons examined attitudes across position conditions and tested reverse effect in right-to-left reading populations.

what_manipulated: role (seller vs buyer/owner) and feature quality (high vs low level construal features)
what_measured: price estimate (selling price vs buying price)
experiment: Four studies assigned units to seller or buyer roles and presented products with superior high-level or low-level features. Price estimates were collected under different mental-set conditions (why vs how focus). Analysis compared price estimates across role, feature quality, and mental-set conditions to test whether differential construal levels mediate seller-buyer price gaps. Mediation by construal level and moderation by feature type were examined.

what_manipulated: perceived resource constraint (high vs low scarcity of time or money) and planning method availability (efficiency vs priority planning)
what_measured: resource-coping strategy selection and consumer behavior outcomes (spending, impulsivity)
experiment: Units faced resource-scarcity scenarios requiring coping strategies. Studies manipulated constraint level and measured whether units chose efficiency planning (stretching resource) or priority planning (sacrificing goals). Multiple methods tested both financial and time constraints. Analysis examined how strategy choices varied with constraint severity, examined perceived savings and costs, and tested whether budgeting moderated relationships between constraint and dysfunctional behaviors.

what_manipulated: presence of own smartphone (visible vs removed/unavailable)
what_measured: available cognitive capacity; secondary: cognitive task performance
experiment: Two experiments exposed units to the presence or absence of their own smartphone during unrelated cognitive tasks. Cognitive capacity was measured through performance on working-memory or attention tasks. The study predicted and tested whether mere presence occupies cognitive resources, with moderation by smartphone dependence (measured individual difference). Comparisons examined task performance and available capacity across smartphone-presence conditions stratified by dependence.
